text: this is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: 这里是BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Neil.
zh: 我是尼尔。

text: And I'm Pippa.
zh: 我是皮帕。

text: Whatever you think of social media, in less than a generation it has changed the internet.
zh: 无论你怎么看社交媒体,不到一代人的时间,它就已经改变了互联网。

text: Facebook, Twitter, now known as X, and in the early days, Myspace, used to be places to post messages to friends or share photos of your breakfast.
zh: Facebook、现在改名为X的Twitter,以及早期的Myspace,过去都是用来给朋友发消息或分享早餐照片的地方。

text: But with the The rise of AI, the fun of connecting with friends, changed into something much stranger.
zh: 但随着人工智能的兴起,与朋友联系的乐趣变成了更奇怪的东西。

text: As Tristan and Asma, presenters of BBC World Service programme, The Global Story, found out.
zh: BBC世界服务节目《The Global Story》的主持人特里斯坦和阿斯玛就发现了这一点。

text: Oh, what is this?
zh: 哦,这是什么?

text: I think it's a man who's supposed to look like Jesus.
zh: 我觉得这是一个看起来像耶稣的人。

text: It's a man who looks a bit like...
zh: 这个人看起来有点像……

text: Who also looks like his dog.
zh: 也像他自己的狗。

text: He's been AI'd, right?
zh: 这是用AI做的吧?

text: Has to be AI'd.
zh: 肯定是AI生成的。

text: He's holding his dog and his face looks like his dog.
zh: 他抱着他的狗,而他的脸看起来像他的狗。

text: So he's half Jesus, half dog.
zh: 所以他是一半耶稣、一半狗。

text: A half dog, half Jesus photo is generated by AI and posted online.
zh: 这张一半狗、一半耶稣的照片是由AI生成并发布到网上的。

text: It's an example of how the use of AI in social media has evolved so much that today it's hard to know what's created by humans and what's not.
zh: 这正说明了AI在社交媒体中的应用发展得如此之快,以至于今天很难分辨什么是人做的,什么不是。

text: In this episode, we'll discuss how social media is changing in the age of AI, and as usual, we'll learn some useful new words and phrases.
zh: 在这一集里,我们将讨论社交媒体在AI时代如何变化,一如既往,我们还会学到一些实用的新词汇和短语。

text: And remember, you'll find all the vocabulary, along with a transcript, on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 别忘了,你可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到所有词汇和文字稿。

text: But first, I have a question for you, Pippa.
zh: 不过首先,我有个问题要问你,皮帕。

text: You mentioned MySpace as one of the earlier social media platforms,
zh: 你提到Myspace是早期的社交媒体平台之一,

text: but the first electronic message was actually sent much earlier, in 1844, using a series of dots and dashes tapped out by hand on a telegraph wire.
zh: 但第一条电子讯息其实早得多,是在1844年,通过电报线用手敲出的一系列点和划发送的。

text: So what was this new form of communication called?
zh: 那么,这种新的通讯方式叫什么呢?

text: Was it A. Semaphore, B. Dial -up, or C. Morse code?
zh: 是A. 旗语,B. 拨号上网,还是C. 摩尔斯电码?

text: I think Morse code.
zh: 我觉得是摩尔斯电码。

text: OK, we'll find out the answer later.
zh: 好,我们稍后揭晓答案。

text: The introduction of AI has turned some users away from social media.
zh: AI的引入让一些用户远离了社交媒体。

text: Indeed, a recent report found that social media activity peaked in 2022 and has been going down ever since.
zh: 确实,最近的一份报告发现,社交媒体活跃度在2022年达到顶峰,此后一直在下降。

text: Millennial and digital native Kyle Chaker is a writer specialising in social media.
zh: 千禧一代、数字原住民凯尔·查克是一位专注于社交媒体的作家。

text: He spoke about the problem to Tristan Redmond for BBC World Service programme The Global Story.
zh: 他在BBC世界服务节目《The Global Story》中与特里斯坦·雷德蒙谈到了这个问题。

text: OK, Kyle, was there a moment for you when you felt like social media changed?
zh: 好的,凯尔,对你来说,有没有一个时刻让你觉得社交媒体变了?

text: Oh man, I mean, in 2015, 2016, I think,
zh: 天哪,我想是在2015、2016年左右,

text: in the United States, when Twitter became the hub of election discourse, it kind of shattered the illusion that the internet was a space you could go to just like,
zh: 在美国,当Twitter成为大选讨论的中心时,这种情况打破了一个幻觉——即互联网是一个你可以纯粹去玩、去表达自我、去结交有趣朋友、讨论最新文化热点的空间。

text: have fun and express yourself and find cool friends and discuss the latest cultural happenings.
zh: 去玩、去表达自我、去结交有趣的朋友、讨论最新的文化热点。

text: Kyle uses the informal expression, oh man!
zh: 凯尔用了非正式的表达“oh man”。

text: An exclamation showing excitement or enthusiasm.
zh: 这是表示兴奋或热情的感叹词。

text: He remembers the internet in 2016, when Twitter was full of angry political debate about the US elections.
zh: 他记得2016年的互联网,那时Twitter上充斥着关于美国大选的愤怒政治争论。

text: For Kyle, this shattered the illusion that the internet was fun.
zh: 对凯尔来说,这打破了互联网很有趣这一幻觉。

text: If something shatters your illusions, it destroys a false belief you hold, making you realise an unpleasant truth.
zh: 如果某件事“shatters your illusions”(打破你的幻想),意味着它摧毁了你所持有的一个错误信念,让你意识到一个令人不快的真相。

text: Kyle thinks social media was friendlier before – an online space to make friends, have fun and discuss the latest cultural happenings.
zh: 凯尔认为,社交媒体以前更友好——是一个可以交朋友、找乐子、讨论最新文化热点的网络空间。

text: A cultural happening is an event that embodies the values and interests of a particular group at a particular time in history.
zh: “文化热点事件”指的是在特定历史时期,体现某个群体价值观和兴趣的事件。

text: For example, the hit television show Game of Thrones was a cultural happening for millennials in the 2010s.
zh: 例如,热门电视剧《权力的游戏》在2010年代就是千禧一代的一个文化热点。

text: So, what's the future of social media?
zh: 那么,社交媒体的未来会是什么样?

text: Will the amount of AI -generated content continue to grow.
zh: AI生成内容的数量会继续增长吗?

text: Here's Kyle Chaker again sharing his thoughts with BBC World Service's The Global Story.
zh: 我们再次听听凯尔·查克在BBC世界服务《The Global Story》节目中分享的看法。

text: We see the large social networks and the tech behemoths leaning into generated content and I think users don't actually want that.
zh: 我们看到大型社交网络和科技巨头都在倾向于生成式内容,但我认为用户其实并不想要这些。

text: I think it's a novelty for now and people will drift away from it.
zh: 我觉得这只是暂时的新鲜感,人们会慢慢远离它。

text: So instead I see people gravitating towards smaller online spaces connecting more more closely with individual voices like creator types,
zh: 相反,我看到人们会转向更小的网络空间,与创作者这样的个体声音建立更紧密的联系,

text: and just posting less in general.
zh: 而且总体上发帖也会更少。

text: Kyle thinks the tech behemoths, meaning large and powerful companies, will continue pushing AI.
zh: 凯尔认为,这些“科技巨头”,也就是那些庞大而强势的公司,会继续推动AI的发展。

text: However, many people want content which is more personal and meaningful.
zh: 然而,许多人想要的是更个人化、更有意义的内容。

text: They think AI is a novelty, something which is new and unusual, and therefore exciting, but which probably won't last.
zh: 他们认为AI只是一种“novelty”,也就是新奇有趣但可能不会持久的东西。

text: Instead, Kyle thinks people will gravitate towards smaller online communities.
zh: 相反,凯尔认为人们会“gravitate towards”(被吸引向)更小的网络社区。

text: If you gravitate towards something, you are naturally drawn or attracted to it.
zh: 如果你“gravitate towards”某样东西,意味着你自然而然地被它吸引。

text: Social media has changed fast and is still in motion.
zh: 社交媒体变化很快,而且仍在不断演变。

text: It's come a long way from the first electronic messages of 1844, which you mentioned in your question, Neil.
zh: 从1844年的第一条电子讯息——也就是尼尔你问题里提到的那个——到现在,它已经走过了很长的路。

text: So, did I get the correct answer?
zh: 那么,我答对了吗?

text: I asked what this new form of communication was, and you said that it was C, Morse code, and that, Pippa, is the correct answer.
zh: 我问的是这种新的通讯方式叫什么,你说是C,摩尔斯电码,皮帕,这就是正确答案。

text: OK, let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with the exclamation, oh man, used to show excitement or enthusiasm.
zh: 好,我们来复习一下今天学的词汇,首先是感叹词“oh man”,用来表示兴奋或热情。

text: To shatter an illusion is to destroy someone's misconceptions, causing them to realise an unpleasant reality.
zh: “shatter an illusion”(打破幻想)指摧毁某人的错误认知,使其意识到令人不快的现实。

text: A cultural happening is an event that embodies the values and interests of a particular social group at a particular time.
zh: “cultural happening”(文化热点事件)指在特定时期体现某个社会群体价值观和兴趣的事件。

text: time.
zh: 时期。

text: A behemoth is an extremely large and powerful company or organisation.
zh: “behemoth”指规模极其庞大、势力强大的公司或组织。

text: A novelty is something which is new and unusual and therefore exciting.
zh: “novelty”指新奇、不同寻常因而令人兴奋的事物。

text: And finally, if you gravitate towards something, you are naturally attracted to it.
zh: 最后,如果你“gravitate towards”某样东西,意味着你自然而然地被它吸引。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but if you're still using the internet, head over to our website bbclearningenglish .com, where you'll find a worksheet and quiz for this episode.
zh: 我们的六分钟又到时间了,不过如果你还在上网,就来我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com看看吧,那里有这一集的练习题和小测验。

text: See you again soon, but for now it's goodbye.
zh: 我们很快再见,现在先说再见。

text: Goodbye.
zh: 再见。

text: Bye.
zh: 拜拜。
